objective: Measure body temperature using a mercury/digital thermometer
student_goal: Student should be able to prepare the thermometer, explain the procedure, measure oral temperature safely, read the value correctly, and clean the instrument after use.

cards.heading: Purpose
cards.content: Body temperature is measured to identify fever, hypothermia, and abnormal temperature variation. It is a basic vital sign and must be taken accurately before interpreting the patient’s condition.

cards.heading: Before Measurement
cards.content: Ask whether the patient has recently taken hot or cold food/drink or smoked. These can alter oral temperature reading. Clean the thermometer with antiseptic spirit and wipe it dry before use.

cards.heading: Procedure
cards.content: Explain the procedure and take verbal consent. Place the thermometer bulb/probe under the patient’s tongue. Ask the patient to close the lips gently around it without biting. Keep it in place for at least 2 minutes for mercury thermometer or until beep for digital thermometer.

cards.heading: After Measurement
cards.content: Remove the thermometer carefully and read the temperature immediately. Record the value with unit, either °C or °F. Clean the thermometer again after use.

cards.heading: Normal Value
cards.content: Average normal body temperature is about 37°C or 98.4°F. Normal range is approximately 36.6–37.2°C or 97–99°F.

cards.heading: Common Mistakes
cards.content: Not asking about hot/cold drinks or smoking, not cleaning the thermometer, placing the thermometer above the tongue instead of under it, allowing the patient to bite it, or forgetting to record the unit.

objective: Examine the wrist joint for functionality
student_goal: Student should be able to inspect, palpate, and test active and passive movements of the wrist joint.

cards.heading: Purpose
cards.content: Wrist examination checks pain, swelling, tenderness, deformity, range of movement, and functional limitation.

cards.heading: Preparation
cards.content: Wash hands, introduce yourself, explain the examination, obtain verbal consent, expose both hands and wrists, and ask about pain before touching or moving the joint.

cards.heading: Look
cards.content: Inspect both hands and wrists together. Look for asymmetry, scars, swelling, deformity, redness, muscle wasting, and abnormal posture.

cards.heading: Feel
cards.content: Palpate around the wrist joint for tenderness, warmth, swelling, or bony irregularity. Compare both sides.

cards.heading: Move
cards.content: Ask the patient to extend wrists by putting palms together and raising elbows. Then ask the patient to flex wrists by putting backs of hands together. Repeat movements passively after asking the patient to relax.

cards.heading: Normal Findings
cards.content: Both wrists are symmetrical, non-tender, not swollen, and movements are smooth, equal, and pain-free.

cards.heading: Common Mistakes
cards.content: Examining only one wrist, not asking about pain, skipping passive movements, or not comparing both sides.

objective: Examine strength of the upper limb
skill: Upper limb strength and power examination
student_goal: Student should be able to inspect the upper limbs, assess muscle appearance, and test major muscle groups against resistance.

cards.heading: Purpose
cards.content: Upper limb power examination assesses strength of major muscle groups and helps detect weakness, asymmetry, nerve lesions, wasting, and functional impairment.

cards.heading: Preparation
cards.content: Wash hands, explain the procedure, take verbal consent, ensure privacy, expose both upper limbs, and ask about pain before applying resistance.

cards.heading: Inspection
cards.content: Inspect both upper limbs for scars, asymmetry, deformity, tremor, fasciculations, and muscle wasting.

cards.heading: Shoulder Power
cards.content: Ask the patient to abduct both arms while you apply downward resistance. Compare both sides.

cards.heading: Elbow Power
cards.content: Test elbow flexion by asking the patient to bend the elbow against resistance. Test elbow extension by asking the patient to straighten the elbow against resistance.

cards.heading: Wrist Power
cards.content: Test wrist extension and wrist flexion against resistance. Compare both sides.

cards.heading: Hand Power
cards.content: Test grip by asking the patient to squeeze your fingers. Test finger abduction by asking the patient to spread fingers while you push them together. Test thumb opposition by asking the patient to touch thumb to little finger against resistance.

cards.heading: Common Mistakes
cards.content: Not comparing both sides, using sudden excessive force, forgetting small hand muscles, or confusing pain-limited movement with true weakness.

objective: Examine strength of the lower limb
skill: Lower limb strength and power examination
student_goal: Student should be able to test lower limb power at hip, knee, ankle, and toes against resistance.

cards.heading: Purpose
cards.content: Lower limb power examination assesses strength of hip, knee, ankle, and toe muscle groups. It helps detect weakness, asymmetry, motor nerve involvement, and functional impairment.

cards.heading: Preparation
cards.content: Wash hands, explain and obtain consent, ensure privacy, expose the lower limbs, ask about pain, and position the patient lying down.

cards.heading: Hip Power
cards.content: Test hip flexion by asking the patient to raise the leg against resistance. Test adduction by asking the patient to bring legs together against resistance. Test abduction by asking the patient to move legs apart against resistance. Test extension by asking the patient to press the thigh down against resistance.

cards.heading: Knee Power
cards.content: Test knee extension by asking the patient to kick out against resistance. This tests quadriceps. Test knee flexion by asking the patient to pull heel toward buttock against resistance. This tests hamstrings.

cards.heading: Ankle and Toe Power
cards.content: Test dorsiflexion by asking the patient to pull foot upward. Test plantarflexion by asking the patient to push downward like pressing a pedal. Test big toe extension by asking the patient to lift the big toe upward against resistance.

cards.heading: Common Mistakes
cards.content: Not stabilizing the limb, testing only one side, forgetting ankle and big toe movements, or applying unequal resistance.

objective: Examine the knee joint for functionality
student_goal: Student should be able to inspect, palpate, measure, and test movement and swelling of the knee joint.

cards.heading: Purpose
cards.content: Knee examination assesses alignment, swelling, tenderness, temperature, quadriceps wasting, range of movement, crepitus, and effusion.

cards.heading: Preparation
cards.content: Wash hands, explain the procedure, take consent, expose both knees while maintaining privacy, ask about pain, and position the patient supine.

cards.heading: Look
cards.content: Inspect both legs and patellae. Look for varus or valgus deformity, swelling, scars, plaques, erythema, wasting, and asymmetry.

cards.heading: Feel
cards.content: Check swelling and compare knee temperature using the back of your hands. Palpate joint line, patella, patellar tendon, tibial tuberosity, collateral ligaments, and popliteal fossa.

cards.heading: Measure
cards.content: Measure quadriceps bulk around the thigh about 20 cm above the tibial tuberosity. Compare both sides.

cards.heading: Move
cards.content: Test active and passive knee flexion and extension. Normal flexion is about 140° and extension is 0°. Feel for crepitus during movement.

cards.heading: Patellar Tap
cards.content: With the knee fully extended, empty the suprapatellar pouch by sliding the hand downward, then tap the patella. A distinct tap suggests joint effusion.

cards.heading: Common Mistakes
cards.content: Not exposing both knees, not comparing temperature, skipping quadriceps measurement, or performing patellar tap incorrectly.

objective: Examine the shoulder joint for functionality
student_goal: Student should be able to inspect, palpate, compare temperature, and test active and passive shoulder movements.

cards.heading: Purpose
cards.content: Shoulder examination assesses pain, swelling, deformity, muscle wasting, tenderness, range of movement, and scapular motion.

cards.heading: Preparation
cards.content: Wash hands, explain the procedure, take consent, expose both shoulders and arms, maintain privacy, and ask about pain before starting.

cards.heading: Look
cards.content: Inspect from the front, side, and back. Look for scars, swelling, deformity, abnormal alignment, muscle wasting, and scapular position.

cards.heading: Feel
cards.content: Compare shoulder temperature using the back of your hands. Palpate clavicle, acromioclavicular joint, acromion, humeral head area, scapula, and tender points.

cards.heading: Active Movements
cards.content: Ask the patient to abduct arms sideways, flex arms forward, extend arms backward, adduct arm across the body, place hands behind head for external rotation, and place hands behind back for internal rotation.

cards.heading: Passive Movements
cards.content: Ask the patient to relax. Move the shoulder passively through the same movements and observe for pain, restriction, and scapular movement.

cards.heading: Common Mistakes
cards.content: Not inspecting from the back, not comparing both sides, skipping scapular movement, or forcing a painful shoulder.

objective: Examine the hip joint for functionality
student_goal: Student should be able to assess gait, inspect hip and legs, measure limb length, palpate tenderness, and test active and passive hip movements.

cards.heading: Purpose
cards.content: Hip examination assesses gait, deformity, swelling, tenderness, limb length difference, active range of movement, passive range of movement, and pain during motion.

cards.heading: Preparation
cards.content: Wash hands, explain the procedure, take consent, ensure privacy, expose lower limbs adequately, ask about pain, and offer a chaperone when appropriate.

cards.heading: Look and Gait
cards.content: Inspect legs and hip region for deformity, swelling, scars, wasting, and asymmetry. Ask the patient to walk to the end of the room and back while you observe gait.

cards.heading: Feel
cards.content: With the patient supine, compare hip joint temperature using the back of your hands and palpate the greater trochanter for tenderness.

cards.heading: Measure Leg Length
cards.content: Measure apparent leg length from umbilicus to medial malleolus. Measure true leg length from anterior superior iliac spine to medial malleolus.

cards.heading: Active Movements
cards.content: Ask the patient to bring the knee toward the chest for hip flexion and extend the leg flat on the bed for extension.

cards.heading: Passive Movements
cards.content: Test passive flexion while supporting the leg. With hip and knee flexed to 90°, test internal and external rotation. Stabilize the pelvis while testing abduction and adduction.

cards.heading: Common Mistakes
cards.content: Skipping gait, confusing true and apparent leg length, not stabilizing the pelvis, or forgetting internal and external rotation.

objective: Identify common fractures shown in X-rays of upper limb
skill: X-ray common fractures of upper limb
card_title: Common Upper Limb Fractures on X-ray
student_goal: Student should be able to identify common upper limb fractures on X-ray and describe the bone, site, displacement, and joint involvement.

cards.heading: Purpose
cards.content: This skill helps students identify common upper limb fractures on X-rays and describe them systematically.

cards.heading: X-ray Reading System
cards.content: Check patient details, side marker, and view. Then trace the cortex, check alignment, inspect joints above and below, look for fracture line, displacement, angulation, comminution, and soft tissue swelling.

cards.heading: Clavicle Fracture
cards.content: Usually occurs in the middle third of the clavicle. Look for break in the clavicle cortex with displacement or overlap.

cards.heading: Surgical Neck of Humerus Fracture
cards.content: Fracture line is below the humeral head near the surgical neck. Common after fall on shoulder or outstretched hand.

cards.heading: Supracondylar Humerus Fracture
cards.content: Fracture occurs above the humeral condyles near the elbow. Common in children after fall on outstretched hand.

cards.heading: Both-bone Forearm Fracture
cards.content: Both radius and ulna are fractured. Always assess alignment, displacement, and joints above and below.

cards.heading: Colles Fracture
cards.content: Distal radius fracture with dorsal displacement or dorsal tilt of the distal fragment. Classically causes dinner-fork deformity.

cards.heading: Smith Fracture
cards.content: Distal radius fracture with volar displacement or volar tilt of the distal fragment. It is often called reverse Colles fracture.

cards.heading: Scaphoid Fracture
cards.content: Usually involves the waist of scaphoid. It may be subtle on X-ray. Anatomical snuffbox tenderness is an important clinical clue.

cards.heading: Metacarpal Fracture
cards.content: Fracture may involve metacarpal shaft or neck. Boxer’s fracture commonly involves neck of fifth metacarpal.

cards.heading: Common Mistakes
cards.content: Not checking side marker, looking at only one view, missing subtle scaphoid fracture, or forgetting to assess joints above and below the fracture.
